especially stress fractures and fractures in the athlete. We now  
review common pathologies of the hip. A brief review of the ortho-

Avascular Necrosis
Avascular necrosis (also known as osteonecrosis) of the femoral 
head (AVNFH) is a progressive, multifactorial disease that is char-
acterized by a disruption in the blood supply to the femoral head, 
which leads to ischemia of the bone and ultimately cell death. If 
left untreated, there is eventual collapse of the femoral head with 
significant pain and disability. The pathogenesis of AVNFH is 
generally divided into two broad groups: traumatic and nontrau -
matic. This disease process can affect individuals from childhood 
through adulthood depending on the etiology.
Traumatic Causes
T raumatic causes of vascular compromise are a result of direct 
injury to the vasculature from fracture or hip dislocation. Due  
to the nature of this type of injury, the presenting symptoms are 
usually obvious, and treatment options are limited.
Nontraumatic Causes
Diagnosing nontraumatic AVNFH is more challenging, particu -
larly in the early stages when the patient may by asymptomatic, 
have minimal symptoms, or have symptoms that mimic other hip 
Introduction
Musculoskeletal disorders are very common in both active and 
sedentary patients. This chapter is organized in the sequential 
process that the clinical practitioner should undertake, beginning 
with the history and clinical examination. Rather than exhaustive 
review of all possible conditions affecting the lower limb, those 
most commonly arising in musculoskeletal, sports medicine, and 
general physiatry practices are detailed.
Disorders of the Hip and Groin
Hip pain is a common problem in patients of all ages. The etiology 
of the pain is sometimes difficult to elucidate because it can come 
from a variety of different structures within the pelvic girdle region. 
Patients with hip disorders can present with extensive pain referral 
patterns, including pain in the groin, buttock, lateral hip/greater 
trochanteric region, low back, and thigh.5,49 In general, the hip can 
be divided into anterior, lateral, posterior, and medial pain. Ante-
rior typically includes what is felt to be intraarticular and includes 
a differential of avascular necrosis, hip dislocations, labral pathol-
ogy, femoral acetabular impingement, and hip osteoarthritis (OA). 
It is also the most common in pediatric presentation for pain.  
Posterior pain should include spine and hamstring referral, but also 
deep gluteal pain syndromes such as ischiofemoral impingement, 
piriformis, or pelvic referral. Lateral differential should include 
tendinopathy, spine referral, and trochanteric pain syndrome.  
Medial differential can include sports groin/athletic pubalgia. In all 
• Patellofemoral pain is a common disorder seen in active 
populations associated with pathologic patellar tracking in the 
trochlea of the femur related to quadricep and hip abductor 
muscle dysfunction.
• Lateral ankle sprains are most common, initially involving the 
anterior talofibular ligament, and best managed with early 
return to range of motion activities.
• High-risk stress fracture sites include the femoral neck (tension 
side), middle anterior tibia (“dreaded black line”), navicular 
bone, fifth metatarsal (Jones fracture), and talar dome fracture 
(Sneppen A fracture), related to limited blood supply and 
propensity to carry high loads of stress.
• Slipped capital femoral epiphysis is most common in 
adolescents with femoral head epiphysis displacement on the 
femoral neck secondary to growth plate (physis) weakness.
• Legg-Calve-Perthes disease is most common in preadolescent 
children with idiopathic osteonecrosis of the femoral epiphysis, 
which can permanently deform the femoral head and lead to 
premature osteoarthritis.
• Greater trochanteric pain syndrome is a collection of 
pathologies leading to peritrochanteric hip tenderness and 
pain, where bursal inflammation plays a limited role, and causes 
are more frequently related to gluteal tendinopathy, iliotibial 
band thickening, and abductor tendon pathology.

most likely to result in either slowing of the progression of the 
disease or resolution without resort to joint replacement.
36,47
Idiopathic. The systemic use of steroids and habitual alcohol 
intake are two major causative factors in the development of idio-
pathic AVNFH.31 These risk factors are magnified in the presence 
of underlying metabolic or hematologic processes or disease, such 
as lupus, sickle cell disease, organ transplant, and coagulopa -
thies.
47,36,31 The relationship between chronic steroid use and the 
development of AVNFH appears to be related to daily as well as 
maximal dose, cumulative dose, and route of administration.
73,88 
In spite of the association between the use of corticosteroids and 
the development of AVNFH, there are no current guidelines  
regarding safe dosing of steroids. A similar dose dependency has 
been reported for alcohol-induced AVNFH, although there are no 
recent studies identifying the absolute amount of ethanol (ETOH) 
needed to increase the risk.
73 The pathophysiology of ETOH- 
related AVNFH is thought to be through interosseous hypertension 
via fat embolism linked to coexistent hyperlipidemia.
130 Patients 
with AVNFH present with anterior hip and groin pain. Pain  
increases with weight bearing. Hip range of motion (ROM) test-
ing often reveals a decrease in internal rotation as well as flexion 
and abduction of the hip. Plain anteroposterior (AP) and frog-leg 
lateral radiographs may show cystic and sclerotic changes or flat -
tening of the femoral head (Fig. 37.1). In early or mild cases, the 
radiographs may be normal. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) 
can be ordered if clinical suspicion is high.
109 MRI and computed 
tomography (CT) can detect the condition in its earlier stages. 
T reatment options depend on what stage the patient is in at the 
time of diagnosis. Conservative treatments include keeping weight 
off the affected joint and the use of nonsteroidal antiinflammatory 
drugs (NSAIDs); the outcome may be improved by attempts to 
enhance the healing of early osseous defects at the femoral head 
before it collapses. T reatment with bisphosphonates may slow the 
progression of bone collapse,
68 and statin drugs may be helpful to 
reduce the severity of corticosteroid-induced avascular necrosis 
(AVN) or prevent its occurrence.
111 Extracorporeal shock wave 
therapy may relieve pain, improve hip function, and induce the 
regression of AVN. If AVNFH is diagnosed in the early stages of 
the disease, the patient may benefit from surgical procedures that 
preserve the hip joint, including decompression of the femoral 
head augmented by the administration of concentrated bone  
marrow. The use of biologic factors such as autologous stem cells 
• Fig. 37.1 Anteroposterior radiograph of the pelvis, showing advanced 
avascular necrosis of both femoral heads.
has shown promise in slowing or even halting the progression of 
AVNFH, with some patients having resolution of the necrotic 
lesions as seen on MRI. If successful, these joint-preserving pro-
cedures could hopefully eliminate the subsequent need for total 
hip arthroplasty.
35,51,108 If nonoperative and joint-sparing proce -
dures fail and there is collapse of the femoral head with degenerative 
changes of the hip joint, arthroplasty is indicated.
34,53,131
Legg-Calve-Perthes Disease
Legg-Calve-Perthes disease (LCPD) and slipped capital femoral 
epiphysis (SCFE) are two of the most common hip disorders that 
occur in children (Table 37.1). LCPD is an idiopathic osteonecro-
sis of the femoral epiphysis that can lead to permanent femoral 
head deformity and premature OA; other diseases causing osteo -
necrosis of the femoral head or that are radiographically similar to 
LCPD should be ruled out before assigning a diagnosis of LCPD. 
Conditions such as sickle cell disease, lupus erythematosus,  
chemotherapy, and long-term corticosteroid use can result in  
osteonecrosis of the femoral head in children as well as adults. 
Hypothyroidism, septic arthritis, and transient synovitis can 
mimic symptoms of LCPD.
57 The highest incidence of LCPD is 
in children aged 2 to 5 years and the lowest incidence in the 9- to 
12-year age group. Young males are approximately 4 to 12 times 
more likely to be diagnosed with LCPD than females; there is 
increased risk potentially associated with extreme obesity; inci -
dence varies among ethnicities, with the highest incidence in 
Whites and the lowest in Asians.
56 Patients may present with an 
antalgic gait, either from pain or leg-length discrepancy or from 
knee, groin, or thigh pain. Hip ROM is limited in abduction as 
well as internal rotation in both flexion and extension. Plain  
radiographs are the preferred imaging technique for the diagnosis, 
staging, classification, and assessment of outcomes of LCPD. 
Typically, AP and frog-leg lateral projections of the pelvis are  
obtained; unfortunately, a lag occurs between the physiologic 
process related to the avascular event and the observation of 
changes on radiographs. Dynamic gadolinium-enhanced subtrac-
tion MRI has been shown to be safe in children, effective, and 
more sensitive than radiographs for detecting early ischemia of the 
femoral head, necrosis, and revascularization.
63,101
The treatment of early disease includes the use of NSAIDs, 
protected weight bearing, limited physical activity, and physical 
therapy for ROM. For the patient who does not improve with

the femoral head in the acetabulum should be considered. Opera-
tive treatment may more likely yield a spheric congruent femoral 
head than nonoperative methods among patients 6 years of age or 
older.
83 In some cases, after resolution of the disease, the femoral 
head may proceed to collapse or progress to severe arthritis at a 
young age. If more conservative methods have failed, the only 
treatment options available for these adolescents or young adults 
may be a total hip resurfacing or a total hip arthroplasty. Abduc -
tion bracing was once offered as part of a conservative treatment 
plan; however, the current literature does not provide evidence 
sufficient to support the use of bracing in LCPD.
40,74
Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis
SCFE is the most common hip pathology affecting adolescents.85 
The pathologic process occurs when the femoral head (epiphysis) 
of the proximal femur is displaced on the femoral neck due to 
weakness in the growth plate (physis) ( Fig. 37.2). This displace-
ment can occur through acute trauma or repetitive microtrauma. 
SCFE occurs bilaterally in approximately 18% to 63% of  
patients; obesity, puberty, and metabolic abnormalities such as 
hypothyroidism may be risk factors for SCFE due to changes in 
the metabolic activity of the physis.
128 The presentation of SCFE 
may vary, depending on the severity and stability of the slip; 
groin/hip or knee pain with difficulty walking may be acute or 
chronic. Conversely, patients may present with painless limp and 
external rotation of the affected leg.
54 Limited internal rotation of 
the hip is a common sign on physical exam, and obligatory exter-
nal rotation is noted in the involved hip of a patient with SCFE 
when the hip is passively flexed to 90 degrees.
55,92 Radiographic 
evaluation with AP and frog-leg lateral radiographs of both hips 
are important in the evaluation of a patient for SCFE. Referred 
pain to the knee is common, and knee pain in the immature  
patient should alert the clinician to possible hip pathology. MRI 
is more sensitive than plain radiographs but is not typically indi -
cated unless the radiographs are unrevealing.
62 Patients with  
untreated SCFE are at increased risk for arthritis; approximately 
45% of children with a SCFE are estimated to require a total hip 
arthroplasty in adulthood (i.e., by 50 years after the slip). T reat -
ment for SCFE typically involves stabilization of the physis to 
• Fig. 37.2 Anteroposterior radiograph of a severe, acute slipped capital 
femoral epiphysis.
prevent worsening slippage. Stabilization is primarily performed 
via percutaneous in situ fixation.
54 Attempts at closed reduction 
increase the risk of osteonecrosis and should be avoided. It is rec-
ommended that SCFE be treated as an orthopedic emergency, 
with prompt referral to an orthopedic surgeon for immediate  
internal fixation. Following surgery, gentle ROM can be started 
within a few days, followed by protected weight bearing for 6 to 
8 weeks. After this time period, gradually progressive strengthen -
ing and functional exercises are advanced, with return to advanced 
activities (i.e., sports) once patients have regained full strength 
and can participate without pain.
Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome
Greater trochanteric pain syndrome (GTPS), previously known as 
trochanteric bursitis, is characterized by peritrochanteric hip pain 
and tenderness. This regional pain syndrome often mimics pain 
generated from other sources, including but not limited to myofas-
cial pain, degenerative joint disease, heterotopic ossification, and 
lumbosacral spinal pathology. GTPS is relatively common, reported 
to affect between 10% and 25% of the general population.
18 The 
prevalence is higher in females; it is seen in both athletic and seden-
tary patients as well as in those with coexisting low back pain, leg-
length discrepancies, OA, and iliotibial band (ITB) thickening.
126 
Multiple anatomic structures converge on or near the greater 
trochanter of the femur: the gluteus medius, gluteus minimus, 
piriformis, obturator externus, obturator internus, iliotibial tract, 
and multiple bursae. Gluteal tendinopathy is believed to be the 
main contributory factor for GTPS, followed by ITB thickening 
and, to a lesser extent, abductor tendon tears.
18 Bursal tissue from 
patients with GTPS undergoing total hip arthroplasty showed no 
signs of acute or chronic inflammation, supporting the theory that 
bursal inflammation plays a limited role in GTPS.
104
Patients typically complain of pain when lying on the affected 
side and present with reproducible tenderness in the region of the 
greater trochanter, buttock, and lateral or posterolateral thigh. 
Such pain often radiates to other areas, including the groin, low 
back, and knee. Active abduction or passive adduction of the 
thigh can also reproduce the pain.
114 Because many patients expe-
rience pain radiating along the lateral aspect of the thigh to the 
knee, GTPS is often confused with lumbar spinal pathology. Tan 
et al. found a 50% prevalence of GTPS in patients who presented 
for lumbar surgical evaluation; a significant percentage of these 
patients had improvement or resolution of their symptoms after 
trochanteric injection. Accurate diagnosis and differentiation of 
GTPS from lumbar spinal pathologies is essential to avoid poten-
tially unnecessary spinal procedures.
114
GTPS develops as a result of altered hip biomechanics from 
abnormal force vectors occurring with abrupt changes/increases 
in athletic activity, compensatory gait patterns from hip or knee 
arthritis, or low back pain.
Diagnosis of GTPS is based on history and physical examina -
tion findings, which include point tenderness at the lateral hip, 
provocative testing such as the Ober and Thomas tests, and negative 
neurologic examination; pain-related weakness of the gluteus  
medius should be differentiated from true neurologic weakness. 
Plain radiography can depict greater trochanteric surface irregulari-
ties and enthesophytes, suggesting abductor tendon pathology, 
tendon or bursal calcifications, and other causes of hip pain, such 
as OA or fractures.
110 MRI can depict both osseous and soft tissue 
pathology. GTPS imaging typically shows a combination of peritro-
chanteric edema and gluteus minimus or medius pathology. 12

Ultrasound imaging has several advantages over MRI for GTPS, 
including patient comfort, real-time imaging, and the ability to 
compare image findings with tenderness at the time of the examina-
tion. The most common pathology seen on ultrasound of this area 
is tendinosis, but ultrasound can easily detect calcifications and 
tears as well.70
The treatment of GTPS initially involves conservative measures, 
such as physical therapy, weight loss, NSAIDs, topical agents, and 
behavior modification. When pain persists, trochanteric bursal  
injections done with local anesthesia and a corticosteroid can provide 
intermediate- and short-term relief.126 Other nonoperative options 
include repetitive low-energy shock wave therapy and targeted, 
home-based exercise programs; both produce superior results com-
pared with corticosteroid injection up to 15 months after the initial 
intervention.60,94 Severe cases of refractory GTPS can also be 
treated surgically.
Coxa Saltans Externa/Interna
Coxa saltans refers to snapping hip and includes three main causes: 
extraarticular (either external or internal) and intraarticular. The 
most common form of coxa saltans is the external extraarticular 
variety, which involves either the posterior ITB or the anterior  
aspect of the gluteus maximus as they travel over the greater tro -
chanter during hip flexion and extension or internal and external 
rotation. Usually, thickened portions of the posterior ITB or ante-
rior gluteus maximus tendons snap over the greater trochanter, 
causing the catching or giving way sensation and inflammation of 
the trochanteric bursa, eliciting pain. Due to the distinct anatomic 
location and often visible snapping, coxa saltans externa is often 
easier to diagnose. T esting can be done with Ober testing for tight-
ness of the ITB. Coxa saltans externa can be one of the causes of 
GTPS; however, many patients can present with a snapping hip 
that does not cause pain. Coxa saltans interna is caused by a snap-
ping of the iliopsoas tendon over the iliopectineal eminence and 
can be found in patients who participate in sports or activities that 
involve repeated hip flexion or external femoral rotation, such  
as ballet dancing, resistance training, squatting, rowing, running, 
soccer, and gymnastics.
Ultrasound can be used to diagnose both coxa saltans externa 
and interna; both conditions can also be treated with conservative 
measures, similar to GTPS.91 For patients who do not respond to 
conservative managements, surgery can be considered.
Hip Impingement and Acetabular Labral Tears
Femoroacetabular impingement (FAI) refers to a condition char -
acterized by impingement of the femoral head-neck junction 
against the acetabular rim. This can result in labral changes and 
chondral damage that progresses to advanced OA if not corrected 
early. FAI can be classified into two types: cam and pincer. Cam 
morphology involves asphericity of the femoral head, with loss of 
offset of the femoral head-neck junction. Because the femoral 
head is not round with a cam type impingement, it cannot rotate 
smoothly inside the acetabulum. This anatomic abnormality 
causes shear forces at the chondrolabral junction, resulting in a 
predominantly peripheral (outer) location of labral tears. Because 
of the peripheral location of the injury and blood supply from the 
capsule, healing rates are more favorable for cam-type impinge -
ment than for labral tears in pincer impingement. 32 Pincer-type 
lesions are related to increased coverage by the acetabulum over 
the anterior femoral head, or retroversion. Repetitive contact  
between the prominent acetabular rim and femoral neck during 
flexion and internal rotation results in labral compression, with 
chondral wear over the posteroinferior acetabulum. 6 Although 
two distinct types are described, epidemiologic studies have dem-
onstrated that most patients have features of both cam- and pincer-
type morphology.8
FAI is the most common cause of labral tears; osseous morpho-
logic changes associated with FAI have been found in up to 79% 
of patients with symptomatic tears. 37 Patients with FAI typically 
present with an insidious onset of groin pain that is worse with 
activity. They may notice pain with end ROM, particularly hip 
flexion and internal rotation, and describe limitations in activities 
that involve squatting or getting into a crouched position. Physi -
cal exam findings are primarily a reduction of hip ROM. Patients 
often have a positive (painful) flexion, adduction, and internal 
rotation (FADIR) impingement test, where the hip is placed in 
90-degree flexion, adducted, and internally rotated.7 FADIR testing 
can also be positive for acetabular labral tears.
Radiographs can assist in the diagnosis and include AP pelvic 
and frog-leg lateral, cross-table lateral, false profile, and Dunn 
lateral views. 50 Clinical correlation is important because recent 
studies have demonstrated a high prevalence of radiographic  
abnormalities in patients who are asymptomatic.26,115
Initial treatment of FAI is typically conservative in nature and 
includes activity modification, NSAIDs, and physical therapy. 
Physical therapy is focused on mobilization of the hip joint,  
improving soft tissue flexibility, strengthening of the hip muscles, 
and improving proprioception and progression from functional 
control to dynamic control of the hip. Intraarticular hip injections 
have been used successfully in some patients. Regenerative medi -
cine techniques have become more popular as a treatment option, 
but more research is needed before recommendations can be 
made. If the patient fails conservative treatment, then surgery can 
be considered. Hip preservation surgery aims to treat the struc -
tural lesions causing FAI; however, advanced OA of the joint is a 
relative contraindication to hip preservation surgery because pre -
existing advanced OA is the strongest predictor of poor outcome 
following FAI surgery.8,76
Hip Dislocation
The deep depth of the acetabulum allows for a very stable joint, 
and dislocation of the femoroacetabular joint requires significant 
trauma. Because of skeletal immaturity in children, hip disloca -
tion is more common than hip fracture. In adults, fractures of the 
acetabulum often accompany hip dislocation. Most commonly, 
the head of the femur dislocates posteriorly relative to the acetab-
ulum. The patient with an acute posterior hip dislocation presents 
with severe hip pain and tends to hold the hip in flexion, internal 
rotation, and adduction. On physical examination, the patient 
will not tolerate ROM or resistance testing because of pain. A 
complete neurologic examination should be performed to assess 
for lumbosacral plexopathy, sciatic neuropathy, and femoral neu-
ropathy. Radiographs (AP pelvic and 15-degree oblique lateral 
views) are indicated to confirm the clinical diagnosis and look for 
associated injuries. CT or MRI can be obtained if further charac-
terization of associated injuries is needed.
Hip dislocations are considered an orthopedic emergency, and 
closed reduction under anesthesia should be performed as soon 
as possible. Postreduction radiographs are obtained to confirm 
anatomic alignment. Surgery is indicated if attempts at closed 
reduction are unsuccessful; it may also be needed to repair

displaced or comminuted fractures and remove intraarticular 
loose bodies. After reduction, nonweight bearing is advised for  
3 to 4 weeks, followed by protected weight bearing for an addi-

few days to a couple of weeks after reduction, depending on the 
patient’s comfort level and whether surgery was performed. The 
most concerning complications of hip dislocation include sciatic 
nerve injury, posttraumatic OA, and AVN, which occur in up to 
10% of patients.
Athletic Pubalgia
Athletic pubalgia (also referred to as sports hernia or sports groin) 
results from straining or tearing of any soft tissue—muscle, tendon, 
or ligament—in the lower abdomen or groin area that connects to 
or is part of the hip adductors.
29 In addition to athletic pubalgia 
and core muscle injury, the condition is also known as Gilmore 
groin and slapshot gut; it is more common in males. Athletic pub-
algia often occurs where the abdominals and adductors attach at the 
pubic bone, whereas traditional hernias occur in the inguinal canal. 
Pain generators can include overload of one or more of the lower 
abdominal muscles near the superior pubic ramus, a strain of the 
hip flexors or hip adductors, stress response or stress fracture of the 
pubic rami, and pubic symphysitis.
29 Players of vigorous sports that 
involve intense running, jumping, cutting/slicing, or twisting 
movements (ice hockey, soccer, wrestling, and football) are most at 
risk for developing athletic pubalgia. Its main cause is the hard and 
sudden planting of the feet and/or twisting of the body with high 
intensity.
The symptoms of sports hernia can mimic those of many other 
types of common groin injuries, and there is no definitive diag -
nostic test, making it a difficult condition to diagnose. Symptoms 
can also be vague, contributing to a delay in accurate diagnosis. 
MRI is commonly used to identify athletic pubalgia. Because its 
symptoms can easily become chronic, it is imperative to establish 
an accurate diagnosis and to institute comprehensive rehabilita-
tion as early as possible. T reatment involves maximizing core sta-
bility and restoring full ROM, strength, endurance, and motor 
control of the core muscles, hip flexors, and adductors; these are 
key to successful rehabilitation. Functional exercises to retrain 
these muscle groups should be started as soon as the patient is able 
to tolerate them. Surgical consultation should be considered if 
there is suspicion for a fascial defect of the anterior abdominal 
wall or a true inguinal hernia.
105 Impact or repetitive loading ac-
tivities should be avoided in the setting of bone overload, such as 
a pelvic stress response or stress fracture.
Rectus Femoris Avulsion and  
Ischial Avulsion Injury
A forceful contraction of the rectus femoris can result in an avul-
sion fracture of the anteroinferior iliac spine. This diagnosis is 
distinguished from a simple muscle strain by radiographs, but 
treatment follows the same rehabilitation guidelines, with ice, rela-
tive rest, and gentle progressive stretching and strengthening exer-
cises when tolerated. Surgery is indicated only for dramatically 
displaced avulsion fragments, which are uncommon.
77 With prox-
imal hamstring injuries, occasionally the bone fails before the 
muscle or tendon fails, resulting in an ischial avulsion injury. The 
patient can usually describe a sudden onset of pain at the ischial 
tuberosity after a forceful movement involving hamstring contrac-
tion while the muscle was at maximum length with full hip flexion 
and knee extension. On physical examination, there is typically 
point tenderness at the ischial tuberosity, pain with passive ham -
string stretch, and pain with knee flexion or hip extension against 
resistance. Plain films are usually adequate to visualize the avulsion 
(Fig. 37.3). These fractures usually do not need surgery and can be 
treated similarly to other hamstring injuries, although the period 
of relative rest may be longer to allow for bone healing. If initial 
symptoms are severe enough to prevent comfortable ambulation, 
crutches can be used for a few days. Ice and NSAIDs can be used 
during the first 5 to 10 days to reduce inflammation and pain. 
Although surgical intervention is rarely needed, orthopedic con -
sultation should be obtained if the avulsion fragment is displaced 
greater than 2 cm or if resolution of symptoms does not occur in 
the expected time period. As pain decreases over the first 1 to  
3 weeks, slowly progressive hamstring stretching and strengthening 
should be started. The rehabilitation program must be undertaken 
very cautiously during the first several weeks to avoid further dis-
placement of the avulsion. There is no predetermined amount of 
time, however, that one should wait before starting a stretching 
and strengthening program for the hamstring muscle group after 
this injury. A general guideline is that the patient should experi -
ence no discomfort or only mild discomfort with the rehabilitation 
program. In skeletally mature individuals, the proximal hamstring 
tendon may avulse because in these individuals the weakest link is 
the tendon rather than the bone. The mechanism of injury is  
the same as described earlier. In high-demand athletes, surgical 
reattachment is usually indicated.
17
Osteoarthritis of the Hip
OA is characterized by progressive breakdown of articular cartilage, 
proteoglycan degradation, and disruption of the collagen network, 
resulting in joint destruction and loss of function. In addition to

genetic and biochemical factors, several external factors have been 
associated with OA. These include sudden impact, direct trauma, 
overuse or repetitive motion injuries, corticosteroids, obesity, and 
ligamentous injury culminating in joint hypermobility and insta-
bility. Acetabular and femoral anteversion may play a role in the 
development of OA.
32,117 Ligament damage results in ligament 
weakness, which is an important factor in the development of OA 
because it prevents normal distribution of weight and increased 
stress on the articular surfaces of the joint, causing cartilage injury 
and joint degeneration. OA of the hip causes pain and stiffness; 
patients may describe difficulty flexing forward to don/doff foot-
wear or have pain with transferring from sit to stand. Walking  
often increases the pain. Physical exam findings include pain with 
hip ROM, usually anterior groin, and pain-related weakness of the 
hip flexors. Plain radiography is the study of choice to assess for 
OA. The radiographic hallmarks of OA include joint space narrow-
ing, marginal osteophytosis, subchondral sclerosis, and subchon -
dral cyst formation ( Fig. 37.4). T reatment is conservative, with 
activity modification, physical therapy, and NSAIDs and other 
analgesics as indicated.
45 Intraarticular injections with local anes-
thetic with or without corticosteroids may help with pain relief.42 
Low-dose radiation therapy can also be considered in patients who 
are poor surgical candidates.
21 Hip replacement surgery is reserved 
for patients who have failed conservative care.
Disorders of the Thigh and Knee
We recommend reviewing Video 37.2 prior to reviewing this section.
Pain in the thigh area of the lower extremity is primarily  
related to muscle, fascial, and connective tissue or skeletal issues. 
This area and its muscle groups are also a large focus in functional 
rehabilitation with the hip attachment muscles to the femur and 
the distal attachment of several primary movers and hip/knee 
stabilizers: quadriceps femoris, sartorius, adductors, gracilis, and 
hamstring group. Additionally, the extension for the tensor fascia 
lata and the gluteus maximus through the connective tissue of the 
• Fig. 37.4 Anteroposterior radiograph of the hip, showing severe joint 
destruction from osteoarthritis.
ITB are often areas of knee pain despite origin above the knee joint. 
In this section, discussion of some of the common thigh musculo-
skeletal issues and the knee joint, intrinsically, are reviewed.
Iliotibial Band Syndrome
ITB syndrome, when the area of pain is at the knee, has also been 
described as distal lateral knee overload syndrome. Lateral snap-
ping hip, or external coxa saltans, was addressed in the groin and 
hip section. However, the tension and possible dysfunctional 
muscle function of the hip can cause pain and tenderness at the 
lateral epicondyle of the femur or at its insertion onto the Gerdy 
tubercle. It is part of a myofascial chain, and the fascial compo -
nents are important in its treatment and influence on the lower 
leg and hip. Proximally, the ITB has contribution anteriorly with 
the tensor fascia lata, posteriorly the gluteus maximus (and fascial 
relationship with the gluteus medius), and with the femur 
through the attachment to the lateral intermuscular septum.  
Distally, it attaches to the capsule of the knee joint and Gerdy 
tubercle on the anterolateral tibia ( Fig. 37.5). In runners, this 
fascial connectivity of the gluteus maximus to the ITB enables 
this strong hip extension muscle at the hip to influence biome -
chanics at the knee. Soft tissue components of musculoskeletal 
issues in the lower limb also need to consider the lateral line influ-
ence.
82 This myofascial line includes the lateral crural compartment 
and lateral lower leg and connects proximally to the broad fascia 
of the gluteus medius and maximus. T reatment should also con-
sider the function of flexing, internally rotating, and abducting 
the thigh through these muscle relationships.
In addition to assessing the entire lateral line on exam, the Noble 
test has been a clinical tool in evaluating ITB syndrome. This  
involves compressing the lateral femoral epicondyle as the knee is 
actively flexed to 30 degrees. A positive test will reproduce the pain 
from the ITB syndrome. The Ober test is also utilized in clinical 
examination, as tension through the ITB can be demonstrated.  
Active myofascial trigger points should also be assessed in the vastus 
lateralis (VL), biceps femoris, gluteus medius, and gluteus mini-
mus, which have a referred pain pattern similar to that of the lateral 
knee location.
Rehabilitation of ITB syndrome must consider the myofascial, 
muscle imbalances, and fascial chain of influence for the hip and 
lower leg for optimal recovery. Weakness of the vastus medialis 
obliquus (VMO) is important in patellofemoral pain syndrome, 
yet the strength of the vastus medialis will be primarily opposing 
the lateral vector force of the VL and ITB. These muscles and ITB 
have active and passive stabilization of the patella.
1 Other reha-

massage.
64 This may help with understanding the basis of treat -

Additionally, modalities such as ultrasound, low-level laser ther -

and physiologic recovery during rehabilitation. Off-the-shelf  
or custom-made semirigid foot orthoses for improving subtalar 
motion and the position of the tibia may also be considered.
Patellofemoral Pain and Related Disorders
Pain in the patellofemoral area of the knee is very common in  
active people. What is less often considered is that it is also common 
in adolescents.
89 The patella is under the mechanical control of the 
quadriceps tendon, patellar tendon, and muscles and structures 
attached to the knee capsule. Changes have been demonstrated in

tion can affect timing for resupination in the running gait after 
midstance. As the tibia and femur respond to the kinetic chain 
loading, increased valgus vector force occurs at the knee.
28 Pain is 
usually sharp with activity, usually from a noncontact injury with 
external rotation of the femur. Dislocations of the patella can occur 
and are typically lateral, as are subluxations. Once the patella has 
been reduced, pain at the lateral trochlea and medial retinaculum 
is palpated. Swelling occurs with dislocations but is less common 
with subluxations and not present with patellofemoral dysfunc-
tion. Consideration of a tear of the medial patellofemoral ligament 
(MPFL) may be appropriate if there are recurring dislocations—a 
situation that can benefit from MRI. The treatment plan would 
include possible surgery if there is a MPFL tear from the adductor 
tubercle due to anatomic instability.
Functionally, muscle balance and firing timing with knee  
motion are important to address. The VL and VMO ideally work 
together to maintain the patella in alignment in the first 30 degrees 
of knee flexion before the patella is engaged in the trochlear 
groove. As stated previously, a shallow trochlea or dysplasia of the 
triangular patella can disrupt the ease of tracking in the trochlea. 
Poor motor control with disruption of the VL and VMO coordi-
nation, with VMO activated late in the movement, can contribute 
to the subluxation or dislocation of the patella.
119 Last, studies 
suggest that the femur/hip control effect on the knee demon-
strates weak hip abductors, and increased adduction of the femur 
creates femoral rotation under the patella instead of the patella 
moving dysfunctionally.
20 The treatment plan should include the 
considerations already mentioned as individualized for the patient. 
Closed kinetic chain rehabilitation is better for activation of the 
VMO in squat with isometric hip adduction.
48 Modalities such  
as ultrasound (not over open growth plates), iontophoresis, and 
McConnell strapping or bracing to assist with patellofemoral 
control can help the athlete with pain and muscular rehabilitation. 
Neuromuscular reeducation of the kinetic chain in sport-specific 
movements, in addition to myofascial release addressing the fascial 
chain, are also important in promoting a return to activity.  
Orthotics can be helpful if overpronation is contributing to the 
patellofemoral pain.
Differential Diagnosis of Other Painful  
Anterior Knee Conditions
Other causes of anterior knee pain include irritation of the highly 
innervated Hoffa fat pad. This pain can refer proximally into the 
thigh, and joint line tenderness may accompany it if synovitis is 
present. Direct compression of the patella or the infrapatellar fat 
pad causes pain but can also overlap with patellar tendinitis.
9 In 
the older knee with OA, fullness can be noted in the fat pad as 
intraarticular issues affect movement and pain. Synovial plicas, 
usually medial, often contribute to anterior knee issues but do not 
always cause pain. In the younger patient, the so-called catching 
and the fear of subluxation are key parts of the history. The syno-
vial plica is thought to be a remnant of the synovial septum from 
embryologic development of the knee joint.
The patellofemoral arthritis component of pain is discussed in 
the subsection on OA of the knee. Related tendinitis components 
to anterior knee pain include quadriceps tendinitis and patellar 
tendinitis in addition to suprapatellar and prepatellar bursitis. 
Pain will be in the appropriate superior and inferior areas as well 
as directly over the patella, respectively. Quadriceps tendonitis  
in patients with diabetes can be at risk for rupture, calling for  
pathology where there is fascial ingrowth of nociceptive fibers  
immunoreactive to substance P in the lateral retinaculum of  
patients with patellofemoral dysfunction.
100 Many risk factors of 
patellofemoral pain have been identified. The anatomy of the 
femoral trochlea is crucial for the patella’s tracking during knee 
flexion; extension and subluxation or dislocation all create patel-
lofemoral pain. The female alignment and Q angle discussi on is 
attributed to the connection between the femur and movement of 
the tibia. Anteversion of the femoral neck can be associated with 
external rotation of the tibia and compensatory pronation at  
the foot; this alignment in stance can cause increased force at the 
patella. Common related findings are increased pronation in strike

As to differences in age, patellofemoral disease can occur in 
youth and continue through adolescence; it defies classification as 
“growing pains” and advice such as “to rest and outgrow the pain.” 
T raction apophysitis, known as Osgood-Schlatter syndrome, is 
also well known at the tibial tubercle ( Fig. 37.7). When seen at 
the distal pole of the patella, it is referred to as Sinding-Larsen-
Johansson syndrome. Prepatellar bursitis can also be seen across 
the age spectrum. It is usually due to direct trauma affecting the 
anterior knee/patella, as from kneeling on a hard floor repetitively 
or from a direct hit on the mat during wrestling. T ouch elicits 
pain, and the swelling obscures the patella as a pillow-like form 
appears anterior to the knee. There may be increased warmth and 
erythema. If there is concern for infection, the knee can be aspi -
rated. A knee pad can be worn, and the patient is advised to avoid 
recurring direct hits to the knee for a few weeks after diagnosis. 
Additional bursae, such as the suprapatellar and infrapatellar  
bursae, can cause anterior knee pain and are addressed similarly to 
prepatellar bursitis.
Hamstring Injury
The hamstring muscle group comprises the semimembranosus, 
semitendinosus, and short and long heads of the biceps femoris. 
These are usually injured when forceful eccentric contractions of 
these muscles occur. The mechanism is usually an acute overload

muscle strains is difficult to do clinically with ROM and static 
strength tests. Pain can be present on palpating the area of strain 
(can be proximal to distal) with passive stretch or concentric and 
eccentric testing of the muscle. Many are low-level strains and can 
resolve with conservative treatment. More severe strains will dem-
onstrate difficulty with full active knee flexion and extension  
in prone and usually produce ecchymosis distal to the tear. A 
palpable defect may be noted if there is a significant tear and may 
be more apparent during active contraction. The eccentric effort 
of the hamstring is to slow knee extension and, concentrically, 
knee flexion. Its support of the adductor magnus and gluteus 
maximus assist extension of the thigh.
In the rehabilitation of injuries, attention to the strength of 
recovery while maintaining flexibility is important.118 The strength 
ratio should improve to an isokinetic strength ratio of 0.6 hamstrings 
to quadriceps before return to most sports. Because these muscles 
are mostly acting across two joints (short head biceps femoris that 
originates on the linea aspera of the femur), attention to the effect 
on rotation is also important in the therapeutic exercises selected. 
The semimembranosus and tendinosus internally rotate when the 
knee is flexed, and the long head of the biceps femoris rotates 
externally. The Nordic hamstring exercise has demonstrated good 
results in the training of the hamstrings. Initial rest, ice, and gentle 
ROM exercises are recommended in the acute phase. Imaging may 
be helpful if needed to assist if prognostication of injury is needed 
in athletes.97 Grading the severity of injury and projecting return to 
activity is individualized; no current system provides a gold standard 
for clinical practice. 38 Proximal hamstring injuries involving an 
ischial avulsion have a longer recovery, and incomplete ruptures 
have sometimes been considered for surgical treatment if the 
fragment is displaced greater than 2 cm or if the athlete is involved 
in a high-demand sport. The rest period is longer before a stretching 
program is started. In young athletes, this can be an apophyseal 
avulsion; in older athletes, the tear is more likely to occur in the 
proximal tendon without a bony fracture. Distal hamstring injuries 
often occur at the myotendinous junction and are gradual in onset. 
The overload or sport-specific movements should be assessed so as 
to return optimal hamstring function. Prevention is the ideal 
rehabilitation approach, with emphasis on multijoint strengthening 
and flexibility overall.
Quadriceps Femoris Injury
Quadriceps contusion is common in activities of daily living as 
well as athletics. A hard, direct impact to the quadriceps group 
can result in a hematoma with little or no ecchymosis. The initial 
pain area and muscles are assessed. Goals are to minimize hema -
toma formation and maintain pain-free ROM at the hip and 
knee. Immobilization in flexion overnight is recommended to 
minimize bleeding and shortening/tension. Severe quadriceps 
contusions with large hematomas can develop calcification with 
resultant myositis ossificans. These can occur in up to 20% of all 
thigh contusions. Pain can be treated with active assisted stretch, 
ice, and NSAIDs, although there is some controversy regarding 
their use in the early days because of potential risk of more bleed-
ing. Myositis ossificans can be assessed in the early stages with 
bone scan and MRI; however, initial treatment is similar. If several 
weeks of conservative care have not resolved pain or muscle  
restriction, imaging should follow. Surgical resection of the 
chronic calcified tissue may rarely be in order when there is  
limited function or impingement on a nerve. Full maturation of 
myositis ossificans before surgery is recommended to avoid recur-
rence. A negative nuclear medicine bone scan defines cessation of 
osteoblastic activity.
Femoral Bone Stress Injury
Femoral stress injury usually presents with a vague consistent pain 
with activity; some patients do not present to a physician for 
several weeks because this injury feels similar to a muscle strain. 
Bone stress injury is a mild periosteal strain on a continuum with 
stress response, stress injury, and stress fracture. Standard x-rays of 
the femur are often negative early in the course of this injury. 
Nuclear medicine scan, CT , and MRI offer earlier and better 
definitions of the fracture for prognostication. Not all femoral 
stress fractures can be easily managed with conservative treatment. 
Stress fractures of the femoral neck can be on the compression 
side (medial) or tension side (lateral). The former (tension side) 
femoral neck stress fractures may require surgical referral and  
intervention due to the high risk of progression to fracture. MRI 
imaging is an advantage over the nuclear medicine scan for early 
classification in high-level athletes. 27 If significant bony edema 
and effusion are associated with the stress fracture, consulting 
with an orthopedic surgeon is recommended because these  
patients may not heal well without surgical intervention. Otherwise, 
femoral stress fractures, from the most common to the least, can 
occur in the shaft, lesser trochanter, trochanteric region, neck, and 
greater trochanter. Conservative management with nonweight 
bearing progressing to weight bearing is guided by the patient’s 
pain and usually lasts for 4 to 6 weeks and is often successful (note 
earlier exceptions). Shaft stress injuries can cause pain radiation 
into the groin, hip, or distal thigh. Clinical tests commonly used 
in the diagnosis of femoral stress injuries are the single leg-hop 
test, which is immediately positive in most cases, and the fulcrum 
test, which causes pain in the femur. Any femoral stress injury 
should have a workup for intrinsic and extrinsic causes of injury 
and must include consideration and workup of relative energy 
deficiency syndrome.80
Pes Anserine Bursitis
Pes anserine bursitis is often diagnosed in many medial knee pain 
conditions. The bursa is at the medial tibia, where the three 
muscles—semitendinosus, sartorius, and gracilis—come together 
and attach to the anteromedial tibia. Pes anserinus (“goose foot”) 
is so named for the appearance of the anatomic insertion. Distal 
tendinitis of these muscles can overlap in symptomology, and 
examination of pain to palpation may be the only finding. Usually 
there is overlap of the tendinitis and bursitis pathology; however, 
a medial tibial plateau stress fracture is in the differential if history 
suggests nutritional, exercise, or trauma risk for stress injury.  
Another common differential commonly overlooked is Voshell 
bursitis, which is a bursa between the superficial and deep  
portions of medial collateral ligaments (MCLs). This bursitis  
pathology is felt immediately below the joint line at the MCL. 
Ultrasound and MRI can visualize both the pes anserine bursa 
and tendons. If there is suspicion of a tibial stress injury, x-ray 
may be obtained initially with more advanced imaging in cases of 
high suspicion. Medial-anterior knee pain in breaststroke swim -
mers can sometimes involve these tendons, in addition to the 
presentation of a medial patellar facet injury, MCL sprain, and 
possibly a plica. Attention to hip abduction improvement to 
around 40 degrees and good external rotation and dorsiflexion of

Popliteal Dysfunction
Before focusing on ligamentous and intraarticular issues with the 
knee, popliteus strain in the presentation of posterior knee pain 
should be addressed. The popliteus arises from the lateral femoral 
condyle and fibula posteriorly and attaches to the tibia; it assists 
the knee in descending stairs and avoiding forward shear as it 
works with the posterior cruciate ligament (PCL). It limits exter -
nal rotation and derotation of the knee at the initiation of flexion. 
Pain can occur when the patient sits in a figure-four position, 
creating internal rotation at the posterior knee joint. This is some-
times confused with a posterior lateral meniscal injury.
Intraarticular and Ligament Issues at the Knee
Intraarticular areas of the knee are most often considered in the 
condition of musculoskeletal injury to the lower limb. There are 
degenerative changes of the joint that can lead to knee pain.  
Arthralgias of the patellofemoral joint limit activities involving 
jumping and running; they are usually gradual in onset. Besides 
the patellofemoral issues in youth (see earlier), this area is the 
most commonly affected in reports of knee pain from young  
patients. It is also a difficult area to manage in the adult because 
many biomechanical factors affect its movement and thus add 
faulty wear under the patella with suboptimal position in the 
trochlea and retropatellar cartilage forces. A tight quadriceps, 
ITB, and weakened or inhibited vastus medialis in the VMO can 
affect a balanced pull across the patellofemoral joint. As men -
tioned, presentation of infrapatellar pain at the anterior knee is 
the best clinical test. 86 Pain with medial and lateral palpation 
retrocondylar and apprehension with glides can contribute to pain 
production in this condition. Last, ineffective eccentric function of 
the hip external rotators may allow further internal rotation of the 
femur with weight-bearing activity; it may increase the load  
and alter patellofemoral tracking. Although many conservative 
approaches have been utilized to treat patellofemoral pain, no 
gold standard has been developed for taping, bracing, or other 
modalities. Muscle balance as well as quadriceps and hamstrings 
flexibility are important, as is attending to strengthening with 
closed kinetic chain exercises. 111 OA can also affect the other 
compartments of the knee: Medial is more common, and lateral 
weight-bearing surfaces are also important. Risk factors include 
prior trauma to the intraarticular structures. Obesity is also a risk 
factor: Other considerations include mechanical load and  
increased inflammatory effect due to the increased production of 
adipokines and proinflammatory cytokines in the adipose cells 
and their potential influence on chondrocyte metabolism. Pain 
and stiffness tend to improve with movement and typically last 
less than 1 hour.
T reatment protocols are mostly nonsurgical until the progression 
of OA limits function and causes unremitting pain. The most com-
mon treatment is weight loss if overweight is an issue, low-impact 
exercise, and strength training with attention to gait mechanics. 
First-line medications include acetaminophen, then NSAIDs, and 
occasionally tramadol. Injections—primarily corticosteroids and 
hyaluronate—are the next consideration, along with bracing. Stem 
cell and platelet-rich plasma regenerative medicine options can be 
considered, though they certainly require ongoing research into their 
effectiveness. Currently, the treatments are oriented to pain-free, 
functional movement and not the restoration of cartilage. Unloader 
bracing has been a great adjunct for advancing rehabilitation while 
also limiting patients’ reported pain levels. Research regarding the 
efficacy of glucosamine and chondroitin sulfate as well as turmeric 
supplements has been inconclusive of product purity and consis -
tency of pain relief reported. Low-dose radiation therapy can also be 
considered in patients who cannot undergo a knee replacement or 
who do not want a knee replacement.21
Osteochondritis Dissecans
Osteochondritis dissecans is suspected to be multifactorial and 
can affect the femoral condyle, and the most common site is the 
lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. The most common 
age group to be affected is 10 to 15 years old, and presentation is 
usually a continuing atraumatic intraarticular knee pain. Classi -
fication of the lesions in the knee refers to the involvement of the 
osteochondral anatomy and displacement: grade 1, compression 
of the subchondral trabeculae with a preserved cartilage cap; 
grade 2, incomplete detachment of the osteochondral fragment; 
grade 3, complete disruption of the osteochondral fragment 
without dislocation; and grade 4, complete avulsion of the osteo -
chondral fragment with dislocation into the joint. 16 There may 
be occasional swelling and pain with activity that is relieved with 
rest. Imaging is typically needed to conclusively diagnose this 
condition as several joint surface issues can mimic its typical pain 
and history. Advanced imaging such as MRI can help to grade 
and classify the stability of the lesion. Most often, relative rest  
is selected in the athlete; in unstable lesions, however, surgical 
management is considered.
Ligamentous Injuries of the Knee
The primary ligaments in the knee are the MCL, lateral collateral 
ligament (LCL), anterior cruciate ligament (ACL), and PCL. There 
are supporting ligaments, such as the meniscofemoral ligaments, 
that are important in more complex injuries involving instability. 
The most common ligaments injured in the knee are the MCL and 
ACL. Patients with MCL injury typically sustain a valgus force to 
the knee when the foot is planted; pain will be on the medial side 
of the knee. The injury is graded from mild laxity to complete dis-
ruption of the ligament. T esting of the knee in 30 degrees of flexion 
and 0 degrees of extension can also assist in assessing for concurrent 
injury to the cruciate ligament or disruption of the posteromedial 
capsule. T enderness will be felt at the medial knee proximally,  
medial femoral condyle, joint line, or tibia, with the two components 
of the fibers, superficial and deep, separated distally by the Voshell 
bursa. Swelling is at the medial knee and not intraarticular, as the 
MCL is extraarticular. X-rays are completed due to the suspected 
force that can result in an associated bony injury. This may be seen 
on AP or lateral views. Conservative treatment is most often recom-
mended. Very rarely, complete tears of isolated MCL injuries are 
selected for surgical treatment.
ACL tears are feared due to their long period of rehabilitation 
and functional importance in lateral movement and cutting 
sports. The ACL is attached to the medial wall of the lateral 
femoral condyle and anteromedially connected to the anterior 
spine of the tibial plateau; it restrains anterior displacement of the 
tibia to the femur and internal rotation of the tibia. Patients may 
report a noncontact or contact injury with a weight-bearing  
motion of flexion and twisting (Fig. 37.8). A pop is felt or heard, 
and intraarticular swelling occurs. Some young athletes do not

dropped backward; thus the test may be positive for an anterior 
drawer if the knee is not tested from a neutral position. Com -
parison to the other knee is helpful, as is the sag test. Injuries to 
the PCL typically occur after a forceful blow to the proximal  
anterior leg when knee is in flexion. They are less common than 
ACL injuries and have less significant functional limitations. Plain 
radiographs are usually normal; there is no significant effusion, 
and the patient has no sensation of instability. Unlike those of the 
ACL, isolated PCL injuries have a different recovery timeline, and 
such patients tend to return sooner to sport at their previous levels 
after a conservative rehabilitation program without surgery.
The fourth primary ligament in the knee, the LCL, resists 
varus forces at the knee. It is rarely injured but can be involved in 
complex knee injuries. More significantly, it is part of the postero-
lateral corner of the anatomic knee. The structures in the postero-
lateral corner include the popliteus tendon, posterolateral joint 
capsule, LCL, biceps femoris tendon, peroneal nerve, lateral head 
of the gastrocnemius, lateral meniscus, and posterior menisco -
femoral ligaments—that is, the ligament of Wrisberg and that of 
Humphrey. This complex helps to support the PCL in limiting 
tibial external rotation. The most common mechanism for this 
injury is a forced hyperextension and varus force to the knee. If a 
posterolateral corner injury is suspected by history and clinical 
exam, it is important to obtain an MRI to determine the extent 
of the injury. There can also be fibular avulsion of the lateral ham-
string; thus attention should be paid to assessing the sensory and 
motor components of the peroneal nerve. One clinical test, the 
dial test, can demonstrate increased laxity at 30 degrees of knee 
flexion with varus stress applied and external rotation increased.
T reatment for these acute ligamentous injuries involves decreas-
ing whatever effusion may be present, maintaining knee ROM, and 
providing pain control if needed. Ice massage or vasocompressive 
cryotherapy are useful adjuncts. With ACL tears, the quadriceps 
and specifically the vastus medialis can be inhibited with relative 
atrophy after a few weeks. Crutches can be used if needed to main-
tain weight bearing. Isometric exercises for the quadriceps as well as 
maintaining the closed kinetic chain, as by cycling, are important. 
Bracing for MCL tears may be done during rehabilitation as well as 
later if stability concerns continue. Many athletes in high-demand 
rotatory and lateral movement sports opt for surgical correction and 
follow an aggressive rehabilitation program. If conservative man -
agement is selected, functional knee braces may be used in ACL or 
PCL injuries with some success.
Meniscal Injuries of the Knee
Meniscal injuries of the knee are very common. There is a lateral 
and a medial meniscus in the knee. The tears can be acute, with a 
traumatic or sudden twisting motion in a flexed or extended knee 
with the foot planted. There can be a popping sensation and pain 
with flexing or weight bearing on the knee or a catching and lock-
ing sensation. Nonobstructive tears are usually accompanied by 
mild effusions and may have either a sudden or a gradual onset. 
Swelling is not immediate in most cases. Degenerative tears that 
are nonobstructive have been most common in the older athlete. 
As mentioned previously, there are supporting anatomic compo -
nents to the meniscus (i.e., fibers from the MCL attached to the 
medial meniscus or meniscofemoral ligaments of the posterolateral 
complex that, when torn, can mimic a lateral meniscus tear 90). 
Examination techniques that are most commonly used and consid-
ered reasonably reliable are looking for joint-line tenderness and 
the McMurray and Apley compression tests. Radiographs can look 
for concurrent OA in the knee but are negative in meniscal tears. 
Soft tissue imaging such as MRI is useful in determining the area 
of tear—the anterior, body, posterior horn, or root—and zone of 
tear in the meniscus. The zone refers to the areas of vascularization 
in the meniscus, with youth having a larger red zone and older 
athletes having red, red-white, and white zones that reflect vascu-
larization from the periphery inward. The location and type of tear 
can help in determining conservative and/or surgical decisions. 
T ears in the red zone that are simple can have a better chance of 
healing after repair than those in the white zone. Most recently, the 
trend is to complete a conservative treatment approach in nonob-
structive meniscal injuries in the nonhigh-impact athlete first. 
Exercise and rehabilitation programs focused on decreasing swell-
ing, pain, and advancing strength balances in the kinetic chain 
have resulted in good outcomes, decreased pain, and return  
to function. However, no research has assessed treatment in the 
acute, traumatic tear of young athletes; a surgical approach is still 
recommended.
Disorders of the Lower Limb,  
Ankle, and Foot
We recommend reviewing the foot and ankle exam prior to this 
section; a brief video is available ( Video 37.4).
Superficial Posterior Compartment Injuries:  
Calf Strain, Achilles Tendinopathy, and  
Achilles Tendon Rupture
The superficial posterior compartment consists of the soleus, 
plantaris, and gastrocnemius muscles ( Fig. 37.10A). The gastroc-
nemius muscle has two heads, the medial head arising from the 
posterior surface of the femur and the lateral head from the lateral 
condyle of the femur. It combines with the soleus to form the 
Achilles tendon and therefore crosses three joints: the knee,  
the ankle, and the subtalar joint. The soleus muscle arises deep to 
the gastrocnemius, attaching to the upper tibia and fibula and 
again terminating to form the Achilles tendon distally with the 
gastrocnemius. The soleus therefore crosses only the ankle and 
subtalar joints.14,23 It is essential for the clinician to understand 
the role of the gastrocnemius-soleus complex and Achilles tendon 
pathology. The gastrocnemius flexes the knee and plantarflexes the 
foot but is most effective during knee extension and plantarflex -
ion, although the soleus functions best with knee flexion and 
plantarflexion.14 The gastrocnemius is therefore more prone to 
shortening and tightening and is often a cause of focal pain in the 
calf but also of foot and ankle pain and Achilles pathology.14 Not 
only is the gastrocnemius prone to contracture, but the medial 
head is also prone to strain and tearing. This is often referred to as 
tennis leg and, though less common, can involve rupture of the 
plantaris tendon.19 The plantaris is a vestigial muscle, with mini -
mal contribution to knee and ankle flexion. 23 Gastrocnemius-
soleus strains, chronic calf tightness with somatic dysfunction, 
and Achilles tendinopathy are the common injuries in this com -
partment. If tennis leg is suspected, a patient will often describe a 
pulling sensation and immediate pain and swelling in this area. 
Meanwhile, given the repetitive eccentric overload placed on the 
Achilles tendon by the gastrocnemius-soleus complex, it is prone 
to chronic overuse, and often the patient will present with pain 
focal to the tendon. Rupture and acute tendinitis can also occur

Examination of the posterior compartment must involve  
palpation, inspection, ROM, and strength testing. In the case of 
rupture of the Achilles, there is often a palpable defect. With 
Achilles tendinopathy there can be focal midtendon pain or distal 
tendon pain. ROM should include all joints that can be affected, 
especially the subtalar and ankle joints. It is essential to also  
observe the kinetic chain, whether it involves the walking gait, 
running, jumping, or squatting; the foot, knee, hip, and core 
must all be assessed because the muscles synergistically work

can be performed by squeezing the calf muscle, and plantarflexion 
should occur passively if the Achilles is intact. This is important 
because active plantarflexion can occur even with a torn Achilles 
due to other plantarflexing muscles.

Imaging may include ultrasound or MRI to help detect tears, 
tendinopathy, or strains when physical exam warrants or if needed 
for surgical planning. Please reference Video 37.5 for a review of 
basic bedside Achilles tendon ultrasound.
With regard to the treatment of muscle strains and tendonitis, 
rest, ice, NSAIDs, and gradual stretching and strengthening are 
indicated.
103 For tennis leg and Achilles injuries, heel lifts are  
often helpful.23 An Achilles rupture can be treated surgically or 
conservatively, depending on the patient’s age and health, the size 
of the tear, its location, and the patient’s activity level. Often treat-
ment for chronic Achilles, gastrocnemius, or soleus dysfunction 
involves addressing the entire foot, knee, hip, and core chain.

Deep Posterior Compartment Injuries: Posterior 
Tibialis Tendon Dysfunction, Flexor Hallucis 
Overload, and Medial Tibial Stress Syndrome
The deep posterior compartment consists of the posterior tibialis, 
flexor halluces longus, and flexor digitorum longus (FDL) (see 
Fig. 37.10B). The posterior tibialis originates from the proximal 
posterior tibia, interosseous membrane, and proximal fibula. It 
then turns at the medial malleolus and attaches to the plantar 
surface of the medial midfoot, particularly at the navicular.65 The 
flexor hallucis longus (FHL) originates at the posterior aspect of 
the fibula and interosseous membrane and attaches to the plantar 
surface of the distal phalanx of the great toe.98 The FDL originates 
at the posterior surface of the tibia and attaches to the plantar 
surface of the distal phalanges of four lesser toes. 67 The tendons 
travel under the flexor retinaculum in their own sheaths between 
the medial and lateral tubercle of the talus. The tendons are prone 
to pathology as they go from almost completely vertical to horizon-
tal rather abruptly. The tendons function to plantarflex the foot at 
the ankle joint and induce midfoot inversion at the subtalar 
joint.65 The FHL is also a plantarflexor at the first metatarsopha -
langeal joint.98 The FDL also functions to flex the four lesser toes.
Common injuries are often impingement and overuse, although 
acute injuries are also possible, including tendinitis, tenosynovitis, 
tendinosis, interstitial tears, and tendon rupture. Posterior tibialis 
tendon dysfunction (PTTD), FHL overload (also called dancer’s 
tendinitis), and medial tibial stress syndrome (MTSS) are among 
the more common diagnoses in this compartment. MTSS is  
defined as a spectrum of stress injury beginning with the posterior 
tibial muscle essentially tugging on the periosteum of the tibia; it 
can lead to stress fracture. PTTD is a spectrum that defines a lack 
of appropriate posterior tibialis tendon function and can encompass 
a range of pathologies from tendinosis of the tendon to tearing of 
the tendon. FHL overload is pain in the FHL tendon often due to 
tendinopathy or tendinitis from overuse. History is often essential 
in aiding in the diagnosis of these disorders. For example, an  
increase in running or activities often precedes MTSS, and pain is 
often felt along the medial shin. This is responsive to stretching  
and strengthening.39 Dancers and gymnasts or other athletes who 
perform repetitive toe flexion are more likely to have overuse inju-
ries of the FHL tendon, with pain along the medial shin and into 
the foot, and may report popping and clicking at times as well. 52 
Patients with suspected PTTD will often report posterior medial 
ankle pain, arch changes, and pain that can radiate up the medial 
deep shin.39,127 Examination of the deep posterior compartment 
relies on inspection, palpation, ROM, strength testing, and dy -
namic evaluation of foot mechanics. Patients with PTTD may 
present with swelling along the tendon and tenderness anywhere 
along the course of the tendon, but they often have pain in the 
medial posterior ankle. Pain is often reproducible with resisted  
inversion of the foot and tendon palpation. Pes planus may be seen 
associated with static or dynamic maneuvers.59,65 In MTSS, there  
is often pain along the medial border of the tibia, associated pes 
planus, decreased dorsiflexion ROM, increased hip external rota -
tion, and a weak anterior compartment of the leg. 39,127 In flexor 
hallucis tendon overload, tenderness can occur anywhere along the 
tendon, especially with great toe flexion.98
T reatment often consists of activity modification, NSAIDs,  
restoring full ROM, and strengthening. With acute injuries, a period 
of bracing or immobilization may be warranted. Addressing ROM 
may involve both manual myofascial techniques and working on 
both active and passive stretching of the compartment. Once 
ROM has been restored, it is essential to address strength not only 
in the deep compartment but in all the compartments of the lower 
limb. Sport-specific analysis is also quite helpful to assess if form 
needs to be corrected to prevent recurrence and to help detect 
other somatic dysfunctions in the kinetic chain.52,61 Orthotics and 
weight loss (if overweight) should also be considered.61,65 In cases 
that are refractory to treatment after 3 to 6 months or recurrent 
cases, advanced imaging such as radiographs, MRI, or ultrasound 
may be helpful to assess for bony pathology (such as os trigonum) 
and tendon integrity and to consider if surgical referral would be 
indicated.65,99
Tibial Stress Fracture
The posteromedial tibia is one of the most common stress fractures 
in the lower leg. Area of stress is often sport dependent, and  
the tibia is the most common in track-and-field athletes and long-
distance runners.27,71 There are many reasons the tibia is prone to 
stress injury. It is not only prone to repeated cycles of loading, but 
load can be affected easily by shoe type, training surface, energy 
availability, skeletal alignment, running/jumping form, flexibility, 
among other factors.90 Pain may present as MTSS (see earlier) and 
progress to frank bone fracture.72 History will be essential to defin-
ing if stress fracture is likely. Athletes will often report a change in 
their training regimen, such as increased training, with focal pain 
that often worsens with activity and weight bearing, not improving 
with stretching and strengthening. 90 Examination must include  
assessing for frank edema, periosteal thickening, foot alignment, 
and leg length. Palpating along the tibia can reveal focal pain or 
more diffuse tenderness. ROM must include assessing for posterior 
compartment flexibility. Imaging can be performed to assess for 
stress fracture. Radiograph may still be normal and may detect  
less than 50% of stress fractures. Advanced imaging (MRI often 
preferred, bone scan is also an option) must be considered if radio-
graph is normal and bony pain is found on exam.58
T reatment must begin with active rest and activity modifica -
tion. Sometimes reduced weight bearing may be needed. Once 
pain ceases with walking, impact-based rehabilitation should 
begin. Location of the fracture will also determine the likelihood 
of healing conservatively. Severity of MRI can also help deter -
mine the timeline for healing. 27 For example, the posteromedial 
tibia heals quite well, but if the anterior cortex of the middle 
third of the tibia is involved (the dreaded black line), this  
may progress to nonunion or complete fracture ( Fig. 37.12).4,71 
Targeting the risk factors for injury based on each patient will be 
crucial for return to sport and prevention. There may be intrinsic 
and extrinsic risk factors to consider.46 Relative energy deficiency 
in sport (RED-S) should also be considered in any athlete with a 
stress fracture because risk is high if the athlete is returned to 
sport without appropriate consideration of RED-S. 80 Essentially, 
the treatment must be tailored to the individual.

Lateral Compartment Injuries: Peroneal 
Tendinitis, Tendinosis, Tear, and Subluxation
The peroneal longus and brevis tendons are the muscles of the 
lateral compartment (Fig. 37.13). The peroneus brevis originates 
from the distal two-thirds of the lateral fibula and travels behind 
the lateral malleolus, under the inferior peroneal retinaculum, and 
inserts onto the tuberosity of the fifth metatarsal base. The pero -
neus longus originates from the upper two-thirds of the proximal 
lateral fibula. It then travels behind the lateral malleolus under the 
peroneal retinaculum and attaches distally to the medial cunei -
form and base of the first metatarsal.
95 The peroneus longus and 
brevis both evert and abduct the foot. The longus also plan -
tarflexes and depresses the first ray. Pathology can include tendi -
nitis, tenosynovitis, tendinosis, interstitial tears, tendon rupture, 
subluxation, and dislocation.
13,106 Patients may complain of 
gradual onset swelling and pain at the posterior lateral ankle and 
foot, which is suspicious for tendonitis or tendinopathy. When 
the symptoms continue to recur despite conservative manage-
ment, a partial tendon tear should be considered. When acute 
pain/swelling or snapping/popping is reported in association with 
an acute dorsiflexion-inversion injury, dislocation, subluxation, or 
a tear of the tendons may have occurred.
96 On exam, it is impor-
tant to inspect, palpate, and perform ROM, strength testing, and 
dynamic evaluation of foot mechanics. Particular attention must 
be paid to assess for swelling. Foot structure should be closely  
assessed to look for pes cavus and varus hindfoot.
95 Palpation 
should include palpation of the tendons along their course with 
common areas of overuse occurring around the distal fibula  
and the cuboid tunnel.
41,108 ROM should include assessing for

Commonwealth University Department of Radiology.)
hypermobility and lateral ankle instability, which can lead to over-
use of the tendon. 95 Strength testing while palpating the tendon 
may provoke pain with eversion or may demonstrate weakness. If 
the patient performs repetitive plantarflexion and dorsiflexion, an 
audible or palpable snapping may occur in the setting of tendon 
subluxation.
95
T reatment for acute strains and partial tears is typically activity 
modification, antiinflammatories, and short-term bracing. Then, 
similar to chronic tendinosis, a rehabilitation program consisting of 
muscular strengthening and restoring appropriate foot/ankle me -
chanics and stability is recommended.
41 Conservative management 
is very successful for both tendinitis and tendinosis.95 In the setting 
of partial tears, tendon subluxation, or tendinopathies refractory to 
treatment after 3 to 6 months, surgical referral is recommended 
with consideration to correct not only the tendon pathology but 
also any abnormal foot deformities to prevent recurrence.
22,95,112
Anterior Compartment Injuries:  
Anterior Tibialis Overload
The anterior compartment muscles consist of the tibialis anterior, 
the extensor hallucis longus (EHL), and the extensor digitorum 
longus (EDL) (Fig. 37.14). The tibialis anterior originates from 
the anterolateral surface of the tibia and attaches to the dorsum of 
the foot at the first metatarsal and medial cuneiform.
125 The EDL 
starts at the upper three-quarters of the fibula and interosseous 
membrane and attaches distally to the dorsum of the middle and 
distal phalanges of the lesser four toes.
66 The EHL originates at 
the middle anterior surface of the fibula and the interosseous 
membrane and distally attaches to the distal phalanx of the big 
toe.
2 These are all dorsiflexors of the ankle, the strongest being the 
tibialis anterior.125 The tibialis anterior also inverts the foot, the 
EHL also extends the great toe, and the EDL extends the lesser 
four toes. Injuries include tendinitis, tenosynovitis, and tendino -
sis, predominately in the anterior tibialis from overuse. T endon 
rupture would be very rare, and the EDL and EHL are rarely  
injured unless laceration or trauma occurs.
25,102,113 With anterior 
tibialis overload, patients may report pain with activities that  
require the dorsiflexor to slow repetitive plantarflexion, as in 
downhill running.
121 One should always consider stress reaction 
referral pain to this compartment in the athlete and exertional  
leg pain symptoms that can present here in chronic exertional 
compartment syndrome.
Examination, as with other compartments, should include in -
spection, palpation, ROM strength testing, and dynamic evalua-
tion of foot mechanics. Strength testing must be performed with 
palpation of the tendons and will often elicit pain with resisted 
dorsiflexion or even passive plantarflexion in the anterior compart-
ment. Imaging may include radiographs, MRI, ultrasound, or 
more invasive testing for compartment pressures if there is concern 
for exertional leg pain.
Anterior compartment overload can be treated with modified 
activity, antiinflammatories, and targeted dorsiflexion strengthening 
with posterior compartment stretching. Addressing improper foot 
mechanics, such as hindfoot supination or pronation, and providing 
arch support can also be helpful.
52

Lateral ankle sprains are the most common type of ankle sprain, 
although anterior and medial ankle sprains can also occur. The lat-
eral ankle complex is made up of the anterior talofibular ligament,

43 Sprains of the lateral ankle often occur

restricted dorsiflexion, poor proprioception, and poor balance.  
Extrinsic factors suggest that some sports such as volleyball may 
increase risk, as may the surface of sports participation, such as a 
higher rate of injury on a grass surface in soccer.

present with acute injury from rapid inversion or excessive supina-
tion of the rearfoot with resulting pain and swelling in the lateral 
ankle.

with a continuing sensation of instability, activity restriction, and 
pain in the lateral ankle.
35,44 Examination must include assessing 
not only the talocrural joint but also the subtalar and inferior tibio-
fibular joints (Fig. 37.16).
43 With regard to the lateral ankle injury, 
a close assessment for associated injuries—such as peroneal injury, 
and bony injuries, such as fractures in the lateral ankle and foot 
using the Ottawa ankle and foot rules—must be performed.

is often focal to the anterior talofibular ligament, which is the weak-

tilt test can be helpful to test for ankle stability.

stress films can help in assessing for ligamentous stability by dem-

more useful for chronic than for acute injuries.

talofibular ligament, sprain of the calcaneofibular ligament), patients 
do well with conservative management, including modified activity, 
antiinflammatories, and therapeutic modalities at first to reduce 
pain and swelling.

Osteochondral Lesion of the Talus
An osteochondral lesion of the talus (OLT) is the most common  
osteochondral injury in the ankle. The incidence is not clear, but 
trauma remains the most common cause.
98 Patients typically report 
pain, swelling, locking, and stiffness with a history of one known 
injury or recurrent sprains.
98 Examination may reveal tenderness at 
the subtalar joint. Radiographs may fail to detect an OLT about 40% 
of the time
120; thus advanced imaging may be needed. T reatment 
depends on the staging (stages 1–4) and location of the lesion.10,98 
Stage 1 lesions tend to do well nonoperatively with immobilization, 
whereas stage 4 typically requires operative management.
98 Medial 
lesions (more common) heal better than lateral lesions, but nonop-
erative treatment can fail up to 30% to 40% of the time.116

Pain in the Foot: Cuboid Subluxation, Metatarsal 
Stress Fracture, Plantar Fasciitis, Metatarsalgia, 
and Morton Neuroma
In addition to the tendons of the leg that cross the ankle joint and 
go into the foot, there are other causes of foot pain that should be 
considered. If the patient presents with lateral midfoot plantar 
pain, pain with improper running or jump mechanics, especially 
in dancers, then cuboid subluxation may be the cause.
52 Plantar 
fascial pain should be considered when a patient reports pain 
worse in the morning in the arch of the foot or heel that often 
improves throughout the day and may or may not involve an  
inciting increase in activity.
81 Alternatively, a metatarsal stress  
injury can present with a gradual onset of pain with increased 
activity, focal or diffuse foot pain, and pain that worsens with 
weight bearing; this is most commonly seen in the second and 
third metatarsals.
71 Other pathologies to consider include meta-
tarsalgia and a Morton neuroma. Metatarsalgia should be suspected 
when a patient reports pain originating from the metatarsal heads 
and a history of wearing high heels, jumping, or toe running,
11 
whereas Morton neuroma occurs due to irritation of one of the 
interdigital nerves of the foot as it passes below the transverse

Summary
In addition to a repertoire of physical examination skills, a thor-
ough understanding of the anatomic relationships between the 
lower extremity joints is crucial for determining accurate diagnoses 
for common pain problems in the lower limb. Many lower extrem-
ity pain problems mimic other conditions that require different 
treatment strategies; therefore it is essential to establish an accurate 
diagnosis. Ancillary testing, such as imaging studies, can help to 
confirm the clinical diagnoses and establish the severity of injury 
or monitor for healing. Most lower extremity injuries can be  
successfully treated with conservative, nonoperative measures. 
However, the clinician should be able to recognize injuries that 
require surgical consultation.
